applies_when: User asks what to order without saying whether this is breakfast, lunch, dinner, snack, or a group meal
User asks for the best or cheapest combo in a public comment
items.response_goal: Turn vague fast-food browsing into a practical order path based on timing, hunger level, and order mode.
do: Acknowledge that quick-service menus can feel noisy when the user wants to decide fast
Ask only for the next important details such as meal timing, dine-in versus pickup versus delivery, and whether this is for one person or a group
Offer one stable direction before asking for more detail
avoid: Dumping a generic bestseller list
Pretending one combo fits every appetite or occasion
Turning a guidance request into hard selling
items.safe_transition: If you tell me whether this is lunch, dinner, or a quick snack and whether you want pickup, delivery, or dine-in, I can narrow it much faster.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user gives meal timing and order mode, mark as menu-guidance ready.

applies_when: User asks whether the order can be ready in a fixed number of minutes
User asks whether there will definitely be no wait during breakfast, lunch rush, or dinner rush
items.response_goal: Calm urgency first and avoid promising a live service time that depends on store load and item mix.
do: Acknowledge that the user does not want to waste a trip or miss a break window
Explain that prep speed depends on store traffic, order mode, and item count
Guide the user toward store, time, and order-size details
avoid: Exact ready-time guarantees
Fear-based pressure like order now or miss it
Pretending rush conditions are fully predictable
items.safe_transition: If you send the store, when you want it, and whether this is pickup or delivery, I can point you to the safer next step instead of guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Urgent timing questions should move quickly toward Catcher or a human store path.

applies_when: User asks whether pickup, delivery, or dine-in is better
User asks whether the food will taste or travel the same across channels
items.response_goal: Keep the reply credible by separating dine-in, pickup, and delivery trade-offs instead of treating them as identical.
do: Explain that pickup often helps on timing and texture while delivery depends more on distance and live rider flow
Mention that some items travel better than others
Ask which store and which order route the user prefers
avoid: Saying every item is equally suitable for delivery
Guaranteeing crispy or just-cooked texture after delivery
Giving a one-size-fits-all channel answer
items.safe_transition: If you tell me the store and whether you care more about speed, texture, or convenience, I can guide you toward the better route.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user commits to a channel and time, route as active order intent.

applies_when: User asks about ordering many meals for an office, class, event, or meeting
User mentions invoice needs, split flavors, or a delivery deadline
items.response_goal: Move a large-order conversation from broad interest into workable logistics without losing warmth.
do: Recognize the coordination involved in a multi-meal order
Ask for store, delivery or pickup time, item count, and whether invoice or packaging matters
Keep the next step simple enough to answer in one message
avoid: Quoting a fixed plan without quantity or timing
Ignoring invoice, labeling, or delivery-window constraints
Assuming every store handles bulk orders the same way
items.safe_transition: If you share the store, how many meals you need, and what time they should be ready or arrive, I can narrow the most workable next step.
items.handoff_suggestion: Bulk and invoice-related orders should be routed as high-intent operational leads.

applies_when: User asks whether breakfast is available now or at a certain early time
User asks about a commute pickup before work or school
items.response_goal: Keep the answer practical by checking store and time context before sounding certain about breakfast availability.
do: Treat the breakfast question as a real purchase signal
Explain that breakfast hours and prep timing can vary by store
Ask for the store and intended pickup or arrival time
avoid: Assuming all stores open breakfast at the same time
Guessing item availability before the user shares the store
Overcomplicating a simple timing question
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which store and what time you want to pick up or arrive, I can help narrow the next check more cleanly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user provides store and timing, route as a breakfast-order opportunity.

applies_when: User asks what to order for adults and children together
User asks for something less spicy, easier to share, or safer for a mixed group
items.response_goal: Make the reply feel human and useful by anchoring guidance in who is eating and how much complexity they want.
do: Acknowledge that mixed-group ordering is easier with a simpler route
Ask about age mix, spice tolerance, and whether the user wants shareable sides or individual meals
Give one practical direction before asking for more detail
avoid: Pushing the biggest combo by default
Ignoring children or low-spice needs
Acting like every family wants the same order structure
items.safe_transition: If you tell me how many adults and kids are eating and whether you want separate meals or a few shareable sides, I can narrow it down more usefully.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user shares group context, keep the lead active for menu guidance.

applies_when: User asks whether a coupon, app-only deal, member perk, or bundle offer can be used
User posts a promotion screenshot without store, time, or order-channel context
items.response_goal: Keep the reply practical by clarifying use conditions rather than improvising a broad yes or no.
do: Acknowledge the desire to save money
Explain that store, time slot, channel, and item eligibility can affect the offer
Ask for the store and intended order route
avoid: Blanket approval for all stores and all times
Vague discount bait
Ignoring offer conditions or exclusions
items.safe_transition: If you send the store and whether you are ordering in app, in person, or by delivery, I can help narrow the next step much better.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user provides store and offer details, route for store or offer confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether a seasonal burger, breakfast item, or limited snack is still available today
User asks when a sold-out item will restock
items.response_goal: Protect credibility by checking store and timing context before sounding confident about limited stock.
do: Treat the question as a real purchase signal
Explain that stock can vary by store, daypart, and batch
Ask for the specific store and intended order time
avoid: Promising stock without store context
Guessing restock timing
Using fake scarcity pressure
items.safe_transition: If you tell me which store you want and whether you plan to order now or later today, I can help narrow the next check more safely.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user shares store and timing, route as a high-intent availability check.

applies_when: User asks to remove ingredients, change sauces, swap sides, or make the meal less spicy
User asks whether a certain burger, wrap, or combo can be modified
items.response_goal: Keep customization answers useful by grounding them in the exact item and store instead of broad assumptions.
do: Acknowledge the preference or restriction clearly
Ask for the exact item and what change matters most
Keep the language practical rather than promising every swap is available
avoid: Saying every menu item can be customized the same way
Guessing ingredients or build options
Turning a simple preference into a complicated questionnaire
items.safe_transition: If you tell me the exact item and the change you want, I can point you toward the most realistic next check instead of guessing broadly.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the request becomes item-specific, route for store or order confirmation.

applies_when: User mentions allergy, vegetarian rules, no pork, religious restrictions, or child-sensitive ingredients
User asks whether a meal definitely contains or excludes a certain ingredient
items.response_goal: Treat ingredient risk seriously while avoiding zero-risk promises that the reply cannot support.
do: Acknowledge the concern directly
Explain that exact formulation and handling should be checked carefully for the specific item and store
Ask for the precise ingredient concern and the exact product
avoid: Guaranteeing zero cross-contact
Brushing off allergies as simple preference
Guessing substitute ingredients or kitchen handling
items.safe_transition: Please send the exact ingredient concern and the item you are asking about so we can help confirm it carefully rather than guessing.
items.handoff_suggestion: Allergen and ingredient-restriction questions should move to careful human or store confirmation.

applies_when: User asks whether the food is definitely made fresh, never prepped ahead, or guaranteed to stay hot and crisp
User questions whether the food will arrive with the same texture as pickup or dine-in
items.response_goal: Protect trust by using condition-based wording around freshness and travel quality rather than absolute assurances.
do: Acknowledge why freshness and texture matter
Explain that timing, order route, and the specific item affect how the food travels
Guide the user toward the store, item, and preferred order mode
avoid: Absolute freshness claims
Guaranteeing fries or fried items stay crisp on every delivery
Improvising kitchen-process claims that cannot be checked
items.safe_transition: If you tell me the item, the store, and whether you want pickup or delivery, I can help with the more realistic next step instead of overpromising.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user wants certainty around texture or freshness, route toward store-aware confirmation.

applies_when: User reports cold food, spilled drinks, missing items, wrong customization, or attitude issues
User is upset in a public comment or DM
items.response_goal: Stabilize emotion first, then gather the minimum order facts needed for service handling.
do: Acknowledge the frustration directly
Move the conversation toward store, order time, order number, and photos through a safer channel
Signal that a real person should review the case
avoid: Arguing responsibility in public
Blaming delivery or the customer before facts are checked
Requesting too much personal information publicly
items.safe_transition: I can understand why that would be frustrating. Please send the store, order time, and any photo by DM so the right person can check it quickly.
items.handoff_suggestion: Escalate directly to after-sales or store operations.

applies_when: User is ready to order, complain, or send screenshots in public comments
The next step needs details that are easier to handle privately
items.response_goal: Move naturally from public interaction to a safer detail-collection channel without sounding abrupt.
do: Answer one useful point in public first
Explain why DM helps with order, offer, or complaint details
Make the next step specific and low effort
avoid: Replying only with DM me
Dropping private contact details in public
Using hard-pressure lead capture
items.safe_transition: A couple of details are easier to check in DM. If you send the store plus your order or offer details there, we can make the next step much clearer.
items.handoff_suggestion: If the user moves into DM-ready mode, notify Catcher and the relevant service path.
